bio.bio_blurb: New York attorney general and advocate
bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Samuel Austin Talcott. Cloudinary.

narrative_blocks.header: He beat Daniel Webster at the Supreme Court
narrative_blocks.body: Arguing a landmark trusts-and-perpetuities case before the U.S. Supreme Court, Talcott prevailed against Daniel Webster himself. The dispute, over the Sailors' Snug Harbor estate, was a stage few country-trained lawyers ever reached. The victory sealed his standing among the era's great advocates.

narrative_blocks.header: Eight years as New York's attorney general
narrative_blocks.body: Appointed attorney general of New York in 1821, Talcott held the office for eight years. He was a leading member of the Albany Regency, the machine that ran the state for the Van Buren faction. Few lawyers argued more often before its highest courts.

narrative_blocks.header: From a country office to the front rank
narrative_blocks.body: Talcott studied law in the office of Gold and Sill and was admitted to the bar in 1812. He built his practice outward from Lowville to Utica before reaching New York City. Within a decade he stood among the most respected advocates in the state.

In 1829 Talcott resigned the attorney generalship under the phrase “irregular habits.” It was the period's polite euphemism for a drinking problem. The brilliance of his public career ran alongside that private struggle.

narrative_blocks.header: The bar said his learning was never surpassed
narrative_blocks.body: At his death the New York Bar gathered to mourn him in the Supreme Court chamber. Its resolution praised talent and scholarship “rarely equaled and never surpassed” at the bar of the state. He was forty-six.

bio.bio_blurb: New York attorney general and advocate
bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Samuel Austin Talcott. Cloudinary.

The dispute, over the Sailors' Snug Harbor estate, was a stage few country-trained lawyers ever reached. The victory sealed his standing among the era's great advocates.

narrative_blocks.header: Eight years as New York's attorney general
narrative_blocks.body: Appointed attorney general of New York in 1821, Talcott held the office for eight years. He was a leading member of the Albany Regency, the machine that ran the state for the Van Buren faction. Few lawyers argued more often before its highest courts.

narrative_blocks.header: From a country office to the front rank
narrative_blocks.body: Talcott studied law in the office of Gold and Sill and was admitted to the bar in 1812. He built his practice outward from Lowville to Utica before reaching New York City. Within a decade he stood among the most respected advocates in the state.

In 1829 Talcott resigned the attorney generalship under the phrase “irregular habits.” It was the period's polite euphemism for a drinking problem. The brilliance of his public career ran alongside that private struggle.

narrative_blocks.header: The bar said his learning was never surpassed
narrative_blocks.body: At his death the New York Bar gathered to mourn him in the Supreme Court chamber. Its resolution praised talent and scholarship “rarely equaled and never surpassed” at the bar of the state. He was forty-six.

narrative_blocks.header: He bought the stones meant for a church
narrative_blocks.body: When Hartford's Episcopalians began their first church in 1762, the foundation stones ended up in Samuel Talcott's house instead. He had not led the mob that wrecked the work, but he bought the stones from a man with no right to sell them. The churchmen never forgave him for it.

narrative_blocks.header: “The gates of hell shall not prevail”
narrative_blocks.body: In 1786 the Episcopalians tried again, and at the laying of the cornerstone one of their leaders rose to speak. “I lay the corner-stone of the first Episcopal Church in Hartford,” he declared, “and Sam Talcott and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it.” His name had become a byword for the opposition.

narrative_blocks.header: His will named only three of his children
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel and Mabel raised a houseful of children, several of whom died young. When he made his will, he named only the three who outlived him: his son Samuel, his daughter Mary Watson, and his daughter Jerusha. The document quietly records how much a colonial family could lose.

INST043.name: Williams College
INST043.notes: Private liberal arts college founded 1793 with funds from the estate of Ephraim Williams, a colonist killed in the French and Indian War (1755). One of the original "Little Three" liberal arts colleges.
